mnemonics.title: TASKS FORM
mnemonics.content: TASKS FORM: Task creation → Allocation by runtime → Synchronization points → Keywords (task/taskwait) → Execution order → Recursive patterns → Memory scope. Remembers key workflow elements

mnemonics.title: PST Order
mnemonics.content: Parallel region → Single thread → Task spawning. Remember pragma sequence: #pragma omp parallel → #pragma omp single → #pragma omp task

mnemonics.title: WAIT Before Merge
mnemonics.content: Use taskwait like punctuation: Colon (:) after tasks → Semicolon (;) before merging results. Visualize: TaskA; TaskB; taskwait; FinalResult;

mnemonics.title: DAG Rules
mnemonics.content: DAG = Dependencies Always Graph-structured. Visualize tasks as graph nodes with depend arrows (in/out/inout)

acronyms.usage: Remember tasking excels for recursive algorithms like quicksort
Task execution order depends on dependencies, not creation sequence
taskwait waits for child tasks but not descendant generations unless using taskgroup

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a fractal tree: Each task branch can spawn new twigs (child tasks) recursively
Picture dominoes with colored flags - only dominoes with matching flag colors trigger falls (data dependencies)
Visualize threads as chefs - idle chefs steal ingredients (tasks) from busy colleagues' counters
Imagine neighbors (threads) fighting over mailbox (cache line) while ignoring actual letters (data elements)

analogies.analogy: Like Uber (tasks) vs Bus (loops): Uber handles random individual trips, Bus follows fixed routes but needs full occupancy
Like IKEA furniture assembly: Task B (attach legs) can't start until Task A (unpack screws) completes
Like air traffic control: Tasks are planes landing in parallel runways (threads) with dependency-based sequencing
